stem: For the quadratic equation \( ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = 0 \), what is the value of the discriminant \( b^{2} - 4ac \)?
solution: The discriminant of \( ax^{2} + bx + c \) is \( b^{2} - 4ac = ([[b]])^{2} - 4([[a]])([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds \( 4ac \) instead of subtracting it; the discriminant is \( b^{2} - 4ac \).
This computes \( 4ac - b^{2} \), the negative of the discriminant.
This uses a coefficient of 2 instead of 4 in the \( 4ac \) term.
This is off by one from the correct discriminant.
This is off by one from the correct discriminant.
This is off by two from the correct discriminant.

stem: For what value of \( k \) does the equation \( x^{2} + ([[b]])x + k = 0 \) have exactly one repeated real root?
solution: A repeated root occurs exactly when the discriminant is zero: \( ([[b]])^{2} - 4k = 0 \), so \( k = \dfrac{([[b]])^{2}}{4} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 4 after setting the discriminant to zero.
This divides by 2 instead of 4.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( k \).
This is off by four from the correct value of \( k \).

stem: What is the smallest integer value of \( k \) for which \( x^{2} + ([[b]])x + k = 0 \) has NO real roots?
solution: There are no real roots exactly when the discriminant is negative: \( ([[b]])^{2} - 4k < 0 \), i.e. \( k > \dfrac{([[b]])^{2}}{4} \). The smallest integer satisfying this is \( \left\lfloor \dfrac{([[b]])^{2}}{4} \right\rfloor + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the largest integer for which real roots STILL exist, not the smallest with no real roots.
This rounds up instead of adding one past the floor, which fails when \( b^2/4 \) is already an integer.
This is one less than the smallest valid integer.
This is larger than the correct threshold.
This is smaller than the correct threshold.
This is larger than the correct threshold.

stem: The quadratic \( ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) = 0 \) has two distinct real roots. Using the discriminant, what is the positive difference between the two roots?
solution: The roots of \( ax^2+bx+c \) are \( \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{D}}{2a} \) where \( D = b^2-4ac \), so their positive difference is \( \dfrac{\sqrt{D}}{|a|} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( |a| \) after taking the square root of the discriminant.
This divides by \( 2a \) instead of \( a \); the two \( 2a \) denominators cancel one factor of 2 when subtracting the roots.
This reports the discriminant itself rather than its square root divided by \( |a| \).
This is off by one from the correct difference.
This is off by one from the correct difference.
This is off by two from the correct difference.
